common.tagline: Fifteen variables describing how you decide, collaborate and survive in the lab.
common.entertainmentDisclaimer: A fictional, for-entertainment instrument. Not a scientific, clinical, or employment assessment.

landing.title: LBTI
landing.subtitle: Fifteen variables describing how you decide, collaborate and survive in the lab.
landing.lede: Answer grounded questions about biological experiments, materials characterisation, bioinformatics, and theoretical biophysics, then watch your responses travel through a beautiful analysis pipeline — feature vector, normalisation, projection, nearest prototype — until a fictional research archetype is annotated onto your profile.
landing.noAccount: No account, no upload, no tracking
landing.deterministic: Deterministic — the same answers always give the same result
landing.pipelineFlow: Answers → weighted features → normalised dimensions → prototype distances → stability → your archetype
landing.featureScienceBody: PCA, clustering, distances and resampling are genuinely computed in your browser. The archetypes are fiction; the arithmetic is not.
landing.featurePrivacyBody: Your answers live in localStorage and can be erased with one button. No server ever sees them.
landing.featurePlayBody: Explore fifteen dimensions, an ML lab of interactive visualisations, and a share card you can export.

intro.title: Before you begin
intro.whatBody: A playful, fictional map of how you tend to work across experiments, evidence, computation, modelling, collaboration and staying sane. The examples lean toward biology, materials science, bioinformatics and theoretical biophysics, but the result is still a broad research-work archetype.
intro.whatNotBody: It is not a validated psychometric test, a diagnosis, an intelligence ranking, or anything an employer should use. There are no right answers and nothing here measures your worth.
intro.howStep1: You answer around three dozen statements on a five-point scale.
intro.howStep2: Your answers become a fifteen-dimensional profile through documented weighted arithmetic.
intro.howStep3: A short analysis pipeline projects, clusters and resamples that profile — all in your browser.
intro.howStep4: You are matched to the nearest fictional archetype and shown exactly why.
intro.scaleBody: For each statement, choose how much it sounds like you — from strongly disagree to strongly agree. 'It depends' is a real, useful answer.
intro.privacyNote: Your answers are saved only on this device and can be deleted at any time.

context.title: Set your context
context.subtitle: This only tailors the wording of your result. It is optional, stored locally, and never transmitted.
context.note: No names, institutions or free text are ever collected — just these two loose categories.

question.instruction: How much does this sound like you?
question.autosave: Progress saves automatically on this device.

examples.q01: For example: defining whether a signalling perturbation should change migration, whether surface roughness should alter adhesion, or which term in a membrane model should dominate before collecting data.
examples.q02: For example: browsing fluorescence microscopy fields, flow-cytometry gates, SEM micrographs, spectra, RNA-seq counts, or simulation trajectories until an unexpected structure suggests the next question.
examples.q03: For example: a classifier predicts cell state, protein localisation, or material phase accurately, but you still want to know which features or parameters carry the signal.
examples.q04: For example: using a strong predictor of phenotype, binding, segmentation quality, or mechanical property even while the mechanism remains partly opaque.
examples.q05: For example: spending time on one gel, imaging series, cytometry panel, sequencing dataset, or simulation ensemble until the controls and caveats are clear.
examples.q06: For example: starting a staining variant, a new microscopy assay, a materials synthesis condition, or a new model architecture while the previous analysis is still open.
examples.q07: For example: adding loading controls, unstained and compensation controls, batch controls, calibration beads, blank substrates, negative controls, or replicate simulations before trusting the claim.
examples.q08: For example: a crisp western blot, clean flow separation, beautiful fluorescence image, convincing SEM field, or tidy embedding can feel persuasive before you have checked every caveat.
examples.q09: For example: preferring quantified band intensity, event counts, fluorescence ratios, roughness metrics, confidence intervals, or fitted rate constants over an intuitive impression.
examples.q10: For example: a coherent pathway story, plausible materials mechanism, or elegant biophysical narrative can sway you before the full statistical support is on the table.
examples.q11: For example: when gel bands, flow cytometry, fluorescence microscopy, sequencing counts, and model fits disagree, you try to understand the disagreement instead of choosing the prettiest result.
examples.q12: For example: a marker vanishes after a new antibody lot, a batch-correction choice, a segmentation rerun, a different substrate preparation, or a longer simulation.
examples.q13: For example: setting controls, sample sizes, acquisition settings, metadata fields, preprocessing, model equations, and failure criteria before touching samples or code.
examples.q14: For example: adjusting incubation time, gating strategy, microscope settings, mesh resolution, or pipeline parameters while the experiment or analysis is already running.
examples.q15: For example: preferring one carefully controlled imaging, flow, gel, SEM, perturbation, or simulation study over a broad but shallow screen.
examples.q16: For example: running a small pilot, quick segmentation notebook, rough assay, first-pass model, or prototype analysis so the next iteration has something real to respond to.
examples.q17: For example: ambiguous sample IDs, missing exposure settings, unlabeled gel lanes, undocumented gating, unnamed model parameters, or folders called final_final_v8 make you tense.
examples.q18: For example: closing a project with cleaned metadata, raw and processed images, cytometry files, scripts, model parameters, notes, and figures archived in a way another person could follow.
examples.q19: For example: your best progress happens alone at the bench, microscope, terminal, whiteboard, or analysis notebook before anyone else enters the loop.
examples.q20: For example: you like linking the person who prepared samples, the person who ran flow or microscopy, the materials expert, the modeller, and the bioinformatician into one shared interpretation.
examples.q21: For example: in lab meeting or code review, you directly name the weak control, bad gate, questionable image processing, overfit model, or unsupported mechanistic claim.
examples.q22: For example: you still mention the missing control or model flaw, but frame it gently so the student, collaborator, or project team can keep moving.
examples.q23: For example: you naturally track sample handoffs, instrument time, compute jobs, data releases, collaborator comments, and figure deadlines.
examples.q24: For example: you would rather own the difficult imaging protocol, cytometry panel, materials characterisation, inference model, or simulation kernel than manage the whole project.
examples.q25: For example: trying a new perturbation, unusual substrate, high-risk microscopy setup, untested sequencing design, or speculative theoretical model because it could reveal something real.
examples.q26: For example: choosing a robust assay, validated antibody, benchmarked pipeline, familiar material system, or well-characterised model before chasing a fragile surprise.
examples.q27: For example: after a smeared gel, failed staining, clogged flow run, noisy SEM image, broken workflow, or unstable simulation, you revise the plan and continue.
examples.q28: For example: you keep replaying the contaminated culture, wrong gate, photobleached sample, mislabeled file, failed fit, or reviewer comment long after the immediate problem is solved.
examples.q29: For example: you can leave the incubator, cluster queue, microscope booking, or model fitting problem alone when the day is over.
examples.q30: For example: a late-night idea about an experiment, analysis, or equation pulls you back into notes, code, or protocol sketches when you meant to stop.
examples.q31: For example: automating image segmentation, flow gating checks, gel quantification, QC plots, parameter sweeps, or report generation instead of repeating the same manual step.
examples.q32: For example: polishing a figure panel, colour map, schematic, render, or slide before the controls, statistics, or mechanistic interpretation are fully settled.
examples.q33: For example: keeping meaningful commits, notebook checkpoints, protocol versions, metadata tables, configuration files, and model parameters traceable.
examples.q34: For example: a pile of images, cytometry files, spectra, counts, or simulations without a clear biological or physical question makes you uneasy.
examples.q35: For example: asking whether a pathway, force balance, material interface, transport process, or feedback loop causes the observation, not only whether it predicts it.
examples.q36: For example: releasing a bold preprint, dataset, protocol, analysis tool, or modelling result now, instead of waiting until every control and figure is immaculate.

pipeline.title: Analysing your profile
pipeline.subtitle: Running the fictional research pipeline in your browser

acquire.title: Response acquisition
acquire.subtitle: Reading your answers off the plate
acquire.detail: Your {count} answers are collected into a single response vector.

qc.title: Completeness check
qc.subtitle: Making sure nothing important is missing
qc.detail: {answered} of {total} statements answered — enough signal to proceed.

vector.title: Feature-vector construction
vector.subtitle: Turning answers into signed numbers
vector.detail: Each answer is centred to the range −1…+1 before weighting.

normalise.title: Score normalisation
normalise.subtitle: Stopping one dramatic answer from taking over the universe
normalise.detail: Weighted contributions are folded into fifteen scores on a 0–100 scale.

project.title: Principal-component projection
project.subtitle: Flattening fifteen dimensions into a readable map
project.detail: PCA is computed from the prototype matrix and your point is placed on it.

neighbourhood.title: Neighbourhood discovery
neighbourhood.subtitle: Finding who you sit next to
neighbourhood.detail: Your nearest archetype neighbourhood is identified in profile space.

distance.title: Prototype-distance calculation
distance.subtitle: Measuring the gap to every archetype
distance.detail: Weighted Euclidean distance to all archetypes is computed and ranked.

stability.title: Stability resampling
stability.subtitle: Shaking the profile to see if it holds
stability.detail: Your answers are resampled many times to test how stable the match is.

annotate.title: Archetype annotation
annotate.subtitle: Writing the label onto your sample
annotate.detail: The nearest prototype is annotated onto your profile as your archetype.

result.matchStrengthNote: A display score, not a probability.
result.cohortPercentileNote: Compared with cached public cohort margins. Higher means your primary type separated more clearly than more cached records.
result.cohortPercentileUnavailable: Available after the public cohort atlas has loaded at least five records on this device.
result.hiddenNote: A rare, rule-triggered result. Entirely fictional and affectionate.
result.secondaryIntro: Close behind sits your secondary archetype
result.cohortBody: Optional: anonymously add this derived 15-dimensional result to the shared public cohort atlas. Only your dimension scores, archetype codes and the loose context you chose are uploaded — never your raw answers, name, email or any identifying detail.
result.cohortSavedBody: Your anonymous cell is now part of the shared cohort atlas, visible from any device.
result.cohortErrorBody: Could not reach the shared database: {message}
result.cohortUnconfigured: The shared database is not configured, so nothing was uploaded.
result.deleteConfirm: This will erase your answers and settings from this device. Continue?
result.deleted: Your local data has been deleted.
result.incompleteWarning: You haven't answered every question yet — this result uses what you've given so far.
result.readAloudSummary: You matched the archetype {primary}, with {secondary} as a close second. Your strongest signals are {dims}.

cross.title: Cross-reading: LBTI × SBTI (not MBTI) × zodiac
cross.subtitle: Your LBTI result is the analysis; SBTI type and star sign are optional narrative layers you choose. They change the wording only — never your scores. SBTI here means the independent SBTI project, not the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI).
cross.sbtiNote: is an independent entertainment reference, not the Myers-Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI), and it is not used to score this site.
cross.sbtiCreditTail: . This page uses only its public type labels as an external entertainment layer, with credit to the original author and interactive developer.
cross.disclaimer: The numbers come from your 15-dimension LBTI questionnaire. SBTI type and zodiac sign are self-selected entertainment layers and are not scientific measurement. SBTI and MBTI are not the same thing. Nothing here is psychological, career or medical advice.

aspects.decision: On how you reach research decisions, you lean {lean}. {sbti} {zodiac}
aspects.design: In experiment design and risk appetite, you lean {lean}. {sbti} {zodiac}
aspects.mentorship: With supervisors and students you lean {lean}. {sbti} {zodiac}
aspects.role: Your most natural lab role tends to be: {lean} {sbti} {zodiac}
aspects.blindspot: A blind spot to watch: your evidence habit leans {lean}, so name the risk before it names you. {sbti} {zodiac}

cross.sbtiTypeClause: SBTI {type} is used only as an external entertainment label in this reading; it is not part of LBTI scoring.

zodiacClauses.fire: A fire sign nudges you to act before hesitation sets in.
zodiacClauses.earth: An earth sign nudges you toward the steady, verifiable path.
zodiacClauses.air: An air sign nudges you to talk the idea through from every angle.
zodiacClauses.water: A water sign nudges you to read the undercurrents in the group.

cohort.title: Cohort atlas
cohort.subtitle: A shared, anonymous cluster map built from everyone who opted in.
cohort.databaseBody: These cells are anonymous, derived result vectors contributed by people across devices and stored in a shared public database. No raw answers or identities are ever included, and individual anonymous cells cannot be edited or deleted here.
cohort.error: Could not load the shared cohort
cohort.unconfiguredBody: This deployment has no Supabase credentials, so the public cohort map is unavailable. The rest of LBTI still works entirely in your browser.
cohort.emptyBody: Be the first to anonymously add a result from the result page.
cohort.cachedNote: Showing a cached copy from your last visit while the live data loads.
cohort.migrateBody: We found {count} result cell(s) saved only in this browser from before the shared map existed. Upload them anonymously to the public cohort? This happens once.
cohort.migrateDone: Migrated {count} local cell(s) to the public cohort.
cohort.plotDescription: Each anonymous cell is drawn in a runtime PCA projection; clusters come from deterministic k-means when enough cells exist.
cohort.plotSummary: {count} anonymous cells are projected onto PC1 and PC2, explaining {pc1}% and {pc2}% of the fitted variance. The plot currently shows {clusters} clusters.

mllab.title: ML Lab
mllab.subtitle: Learn the core ideas behind the maps, then try small Python patterns used in biological data, materials experiments and theoretical modelling.
mllab.noResultBody: Take the test to drop your own point into these visualisations, or explore them with the archetype prototypes and synthetic reference points below.

study.title: How to read this lab
study.body: Treat the page as a gentle bridge between experiments and computation: first understand how measurements become vectors, then how distances and projections expose structure, then how resampling tests whether a conclusion is fragile.

measure.title: Measure
measure.body: Collect features from gels, microscopy, flow cytometry, SEM, sequencing, simulations or model outputs.

clean.title: Clean
clean.body: Check metadata, controls, scaling, batch effects and missing values before interpreting a map.

model.title: Model
model.body: Use distances, PCA, clustering or differential equations to make structure visible.

explain.title: Explain
explain.body: Connect the numerical result back to mechanism, uncertainty and experimental design.

qc.title: Summarise replicates
qc.body: Start with a tidy table. Group by condition, count replicates and inspect variation before you trust a biological or materials signal.

pca.title: Project and cluster
pca.body: Standardise features before PCA or k-means so one large-scale measurement does not dominate the geometry.

ode.title: Simulate a feedback system
ode.body: A tiny ODE model turns a verbal mechanism into a trajectory you can inspect, fit and challenge with data.

mllab.resourcesIntro: Good learning materials are clear, runnable and honest about assumptions. These links point to official or widely used documentation that students can return to while building real analysis notebooks.

python.title: Python tutorial
python.body: Language basics, functions, modules and scripts.

pandas.title: pandas getting started
pandas.body: Tidy tables, grouping, joining and IO.

scipy.title: SciPy user guide
scipy.body: Optimisation, integration, statistics and scientific routines.

sklearn.title: scikit-learn
sklearn.body: Preprocessing, PCA, clustering and model evaluation.

biopython.title: Biopython tutorial
biopython.body: Sequences, files, annotations and biological formats.

concepts.cosineAcademic: Cosine similarity compares the orientation of two vectors and is invariant to uniform positive scaling.
concepts.cosinePlain: It asks whether two profiles lean in the same directions, even when one is much more extreme.
concepts.euclideanAcademic: Weighted Euclidean distance sums per-dimension squared differences, each scaled by an importance factor.
concepts.euclideanPlain: It's the straight-line gap between two profiles, with some dimensions counting for more.
concepts.pcaAcademic: PCA projects correlated high-dimensional scores onto orthogonal axes that retain as much variance as possible.
concepts.pcaPlain: It compresses fifteen personality dimensions into a map that's easier to see.
concepts.kmeansAcademic: k-means partitions points to minimise within-cluster variance, alternating assignment and centroid updates.
concepts.kmeansPlain: It sorts points into k groups by repeatedly nudging each group's centre toward its members.
concepts.hierarchicalAcademic: Agglomerative clustering repeatedly merges the two closest clusters, building a tree of nested similarity.
concepts.hierarchicalPlain: It links the most similar archetypes first, then families of families, into a branching tree.
concepts.entropyAcademic: Normalised entropy H / log K measures how evenly match strength is spread across the nearest prototypes.
concepts.entropyPlain: Low means one archetype clearly wins; high means several are almost equally close.
concepts.bootstrapAcademic: Resampling answered questions with replacement estimates how sensitive the classification is to the item set.
concepts.bootstrapPlain: We shuffle your answers many times to see whether the same archetype keeps winning.
concepts.vectorsAcademic: Each profile is a vector in a fifteen-dimensional space; every dimension score is one coordinate produced by weighted answer contributions.
concepts.vectorsPlain: Your fifteen scores act like coordinates, placing you at one exact point in a fifteen-dimensional space.
concepts.scalingAcademic: Answers are centred to z = (x − 3) / 2 and dimension scores are normalised by total absolute weight, so every dimension lives on the same 0–100 scale.
concepts.scalingPlain: Everything is squeezed onto the same 0–100 ruler, so one loud answer can't drown out the rest.
concepts.decisionAcademic: A nearest-prototype classifier partitions the plane into regions; each point is assigned to the prototype at the smallest distance.
concepts.decisionPlain: Whichever archetype's flag is closest claims the territory — drag the point and watch the borders at work.

atlas.title: Archetype atlas
atlas.subtitle: Every fictional research archetype, with its fifteen-dimension prototype.
atlas.hiddenLockedBody: Unlocked only by a specific answer pattern. Take the test to discover the rule.

methodology.title: Methodology & honesty
methodology.intro: LBTI borrows the visual grammar of data analysis, but it is a toy. This page draws a clear line between what is genuinely calculated and what is editorial fiction.
realList: Dimension scores from your answers via documented weighted arithmetic.
Weighted Euclidean distances to every archetype prototype.
PCA computed at runtime from the prototype and synthetic matrix.
Hierarchical clustering of the archetype prototypes.
Bootstrap-style resampling of your answered questions.
Normalised entropy and classification margin from the distance ranking.
fictionList: The archetypes themselves — their names, stories and personalities.
The prototype vectors, which are hand-designed, not learned from data.
The question weights, which are authored to feel meaningful.
methodology.syntheticBody: To make the maps legible we scatter deterministic, seeded points around each prototype with bounded noise. These are clearly labelled and represent nobody. You can hide them at any time.
methodology.scoringBody: Each answer x (1–5) is centred to z = (x − 3) / 2. For dimension d, the score is s = 50 + 50 · (Σ w·z) / (Σ |w|), clamped to 0–100. Classification uses weighted Euclidean distance with per-dimension importance; ties break by cosine similarity, then alphabetically.
methodology.contributionBody: The contribution scatter borrows the two-axis grammar of feature-importance plots: horizontal for direction toward the winning archetype, vertical for weighted magnitude. It contains no p-values, no significance and no −log10(p) — those would be meaningless here because there is no statistical hypothesis test.
methodology.enrichmentBody: 'Enrichment' here means the relative concentration of your weighted responses within a theme. It is not statistical pathway enrichment and carries no false-discovery rate.
methodology.treeBody: The archetype tree is hierarchical clustering of prototype vectors. Family names are authored descriptions of mathematical similarity groups — not claims of ancestry or evolution.
methodology.noClaimsBody: No validity, no reliability in the clinical sense, no accuracy, no p-values, no confidence intervals, no diagnosis, and nothing about your suitability for any job.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.intro: The most private analytics are the ones that never happen.
points: Your raw answers stay in your browser. They are stored only in this device's localStorage and are never uploaded.

The cohort atlas is opt-in. If you choose to join it, only a derived record is uploaded: your 15 dimension scores, your primary/secondary archetype codes, and the loose field/career context you selected.
We never upload or collect raw answers, names, institutions, emails, free text, or any directly identifying information.
Uploaded cohort cells are anonymous and shared publicly; because they carry no identity, individual cells cannot be selectively deleted from a browser.
There is no advertising analytics, tracking, or cookies by default.
You can erase all local data (answers, settings, cache) with the delete button on your result, or here.
privacy.deleteButton: Delete all my local data now
privacy.deleted: Done — your local data has been erased.

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.body: LBTI is a work of interactive fiction for entertainment and gentle self-reflection. It is not a scientific instrument, a psychological assessment, a diagnostic tool, or an employment screen. The archetypes are invented, the prototype vectors are hand-designed, and the 'analysis' dramatises simple arithmetic. Please do not make any consequential decision on the basis of your result. If a statement here amuses or annoys you, that is the intended range of outcomes.
disclaimer.hiddenNote: Hidden archetypes are affectionate caricatures of common research habits. They never allege misconduct and are triggered only by explicit answer patterns.

about.title: About LBTI
about.body: LBTI began as a simple question: what if a research-profile questionnaire were honest about being maths? It dresses a deterministic scoring engine in the visual language of a data-analysis pipeline — heatmaps, projections, clusters and resampling — while staying transparent that the archetypes are pure fiction.
about.openSourceBody: The whole thing is a static site: no server, no keys, no data collection. It deploys for free to GitHub Pages and can be forked and re-authored — new questions, new archetypes, new languages.
about.creditsBody: React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind, Framer Motion and D3 — plus a small, tested library of hand-written numerical utilities.

common.syntheticNote: Deterministic, seeded points around each prototype. They represent nobody.
common.reducedNote: Reduced-computation mode is on — some views use fewer points.
common.noData: Answer some questions to populate this view.

radar.title: Dimension radar
radar.plain: Your fifteen research-profile dimensions, compared with your top two fictional prototypes.
radar.summary: Across fifteen dimensions your most pronounced lean is {topDim}. The radar overlays your profile on the {primary} and {secondary} prototypes.

heatmap.title: Response heatmap
heatmap.plain: Your profile beside archetype prototypes, as a coloured matrix.
heatmap.summary: A {rows}×{cols} matrix of dimension scores. Warmer cells lean high, cooler cells lean low; your column is highlighted.

pca.title: Principal-component map
pca.plain: Fifteen dimensions compressed onto two axes that capture the most variation.
pca.summary: PC1 and PC2 explain {total}% of the variation. Your point sits nearest {primary} in this projection.

neighbour.title: Nearest-neighbour map
neighbour.plain: You at the centre, linked to your five closest archetypes.
neighbour.edgeNote: Thicker, brighter links mean a closer match.
neighbour.summary: Your five nearest archetypes by {metric} are {list}.

contribution.title: Contribution map
contribution.plain: Each answer as a point: which way it pushed you, and how hard.
contribution.summary: Of your answered questions, the strongest push toward {primary} came from '{topSupport}'.

themeDot.title: Behavioural theme enrichment
themeDot.plain: How concentrated your weighted responses are across behavioural themes.
themeDot.note: 'Enrichment' here means relative concentration — not a statistical test.

sankey.title: Decision flow
sankey.plain: Answers flowing through weights, dimensions and groups to your result.
sankey.summary: This flow traces answers → dimensions → groups → your nearest archetypes, using the real scoring weights.

similarity.title: Archetype similarity matrix
similarity.plain: How close every archetype is to every other, clustered together.
similarity.summary: A clustered archetype-by-archetype {metric} matrix. Your winning archetype {primary} is highlighted.

entropy.title: Profile spread
entropy.plain: Whether one archetype clearly wins or several are almost tied.
entropy.high: Spread — you occupy a shared neighbourhood

bootstrap.title: Stability under resampling
bootstrap.plain: Resample your answers many times; see how often each archetype wins.
bootstrap.note: This measures internal stability within this fictional scoring system. It is not clinical reliability or scientific validation.
bootstrap.summary: Across {n} resamples, {primary} won {stability}% of the time.

kmeans.title: k-means demonstration
kmeans.plain: Watch synthetic points sort themselves into k clusters.
kmeans.label: Exploratory clustering demonstration — not the final personality classifier
kmeans.summary: With k = {k}, the demonstration reaches silhouette {silhouette} after {iterations} iterations.

dendro.title: Archetype similarity tree
dendro.plain: Archetypes merged from most to least similar.
dendro.summary: Hierarchical clustering ({linkage} linkage) groups the archetypes into {clusters} families at the current threshold.

decision.title: Decision-region playground
decision.plain: Pick two dimensions and drag a point to see which archetype claims it.
decision.label: Explore an alternate research universe — your real result is untouched.
decision.summary: In the {x} vs {y} plane, your point currently lands nearest {nearest}.

phylogeny.title: Prototype similarity tree
phylogeny.plain: A radial family tree of archetype similarity.
phylogeny.note: Family names are authored labels for similarity groups — not biological ancestry.
phylogeny.summary: The archetypes fall into authored families such as {families}.

question.name: Question style
question.description: How you decide what's worth asking.

evidence.name: Evidence style
evidence.description: How you decide what to believe.

hypothesis_pattern.name: Question origin
hypothesis_pattern.description: Whether you start from a theory to test or let the data suggest the question.

explanation_prediction.name: Goal of inquiry
explanation_prediction.description: Whether you chase why it happens or how well you can predict it.

depth_breadth.name: Scope
depth_breadth.description: Whether you go deep on one thing or range across many.

acceptance_scepticism.name: Default stance
acceptance_scepticism.description: How readily you accept a claim before scrutinising it.

qualitative_quantitative.name: Reasoning mode
qualitative_quantitative.description: Whether you lead with numbers or with narrative and feel.

result_reproducibility.name: Evidence priority
result_reproducibility.description: Whether the finding or its repeatability comes first.

improvisation_planning.name: Approach
improvisation_planning.description: Whether you plan the whole route or find it as you go.

launch_iteration.name: Delivery
launch_iteration.description: Whether you prepare one big effort or ship and iterate.

opening_completing.name: Project arc
opening_completing.description: Whether you love starting or love finishing.

team_independent.name: Work mode
team_independent.description: Whether you think best together or alone.

diplomatic_direct.name: Communication
diplomatic_direct.description: Whether you cushion feedback or say it plainly.

specialist_organiser.name: Contribution
specialist_organiser.description: Whether you go deep on one part or coordinate the whole.

safe_risk.name: Risk appetite
safe_risk.description: Whether you prefer sure footing or bold bets.

rumination_adaptation.name: After setbacks
rumination_adaptation.description: Whether a failure lingers or you adjust and move on.

fusion_boundaries.name: Work–life edge
fusion_boundaries.description: Whether work bleeds into everything or stays in its lane.

questions.q01: I prefer starting from a proposed biological or physical mechanism before collecting or analysing data.
questions.q02: I enjoy letting measurements, images, sequences or simulations reveal the question as I explore them.
questions.q03: A predictive model still bothers me if I cannot explain what features or parameters are driving it.
questions.q04: If a model predicts a phenotype, structure or material property well, I can use it before I fully understand why.
questions.q05: I would rather deeply validate one experiment or dataset than keep generating new ones.
questions.q06: A new assay, dataset, material condition or model can pull me in before the current project is finished.
questions.q07: Before believing a result, my instinct is to ask for another control, calibration or replicate.
questions.q08: A striking gel, image, cytometry plot, micrograph or embedding can make me trust a result quickly.
questions.q09: I trust quantified signals and fitted parameters more than a strong visual or intuitive impression.
questions.q10: A coherent biological, materials or biophysical story can persuade me before I have seen all the statistics.
questions.q11: When assays, images, sequencing results and models disagree, I try to understand the disagreement.
questions.q12: If a result disappears after a rerun, new batch or different preprocessing choice, I am tempted to move on.
questions.q13: I like the controls, metadata, acquisition settings and analysis plan settled before I touch samples or code.
questions.q14: I often refine the protocol, model or pipeline while the experiment or analysis is already running.
questions.q15: I prefer one carefully controlled experiment or simulation study over many quick exploratory attempts.
questions.q16: I would rather run a quick pilot or rough computational prototype and improve it through iteration.
questions.q17: Messy labels, missing metadata, undocumented gates or untracked parameters make me uncomfortable.
questions.q18: I like to finish a project with the raw data, processed data, code, notes and figures cleanly archived.
questions.q19: I do my clearest thinking alone at the bench, microscope, terminal or whiteboard.
questions.q20: I enjoy solving hard problems with people from experimental, materials, computational and modelling backgrounds.
questions.q21: In discussion, I state the weak control, flawed gate, questionable image processing or model issue plainly.
questions.q22: I soften critical feedback so the person or collaboration can absorb it and keep moving.
questions.q23: I naturally end up coordinating samples, instruments, compute jobs, collaborators and deadlines.
questions.q24: I would rather own one deep technical component than manage the entire study.
questions.q25: I am drawn to risky experiments or models when they might reveal a genuinely new mechanism.
questions.q26: I prefer reliable protocols, validated reagents, benchmarked pipelines or well-characterised models.
questions.q27: After a failed experiment, broken workflow or unstable simulation, I adjust the plan and continue.
questions.q28: I replay mistakes long after the immediate lab, analysis or modelling problem has been solved.
questions.q29: I can leave the samples, instruments, code and equations alone when the workday is over.
questions.q30: A late-night experimental idea, analysis question or theoretical model can pull me back into work.
questions.q31: I would rather automate repetitive image, cytometry, QC, plotting or simulation tasks than repeat them by hand.
questions.q32: I sometimes polish the figure or visualisation before the controls, statistics or mechanism are fully settled.
questions.q33: I keep protocols, notebooks, code, metadata and model parameters traceable through clean version history.
questions.q34: I get restless when a project has plenty of measurements but no clear biological or physical question.
questions.q35: I care more about what causes an observation than about what merely predicts it.
questions.q36: I would rather release a bold preprint, dataset, protocol, tool or model now than wait for a perfect package.

templates.strongest: Your sharpest signal is {dimension} — you lean clearly toward {endpoint}.
templates.balanced: You sit close to the midpoint on {dimension}, holding {low} and {high} in balance.
templates.interaction: {primary} leads your profile, with {secondary} as a strong second voice — a blend that's more interesting than either alone.
templates.stabilityStable: Under resampling, this match held in {stability}% of replicates, so it's a fairly settled reading.
templates.stabilityMixed: Under resampling, this match held in {stability}% of replicates — your profile genuinely sits between a few styles.
templates.entropyLow: One archetype clearly wins for you.
templates.entropyHigh: Your profile occupies a shared neighbourhood between several research styles.

field.experimental: In hands-on work, this shows up at the bench: in how you treat a protocol, a surprising reading, and the gap between a clean result and a real one.
field.computational: In computational work, this plays out in your repos and pipelines — how you weigh a metric, a plot, and whether the whole thing re-runs tomorrow.
field.theory: In theory and modelling, it shapes how far you'll follow an elegant assumption before the world gets a veto.
field.clinical: In applied and clinical settings, it colours how you balance a tidy answer against the messiness of the real case.
field.fieldwork: In field and observational work, it shows in how you hold a plan loosely against whatever the day actually hands you.
field.engineering: In methods and engineering work, it surfaces in what you'll automate, standardise, and refuse to do by hand twice.
field.social: In social and qualitative work, it appears in how you weigh a compelling account against a countable pattern.
field.other: Whatever your exact field, it shapes how you decide what's worth doing and what's worth believing.

stage.undergrad: This early, the pattern is a tendency, not a fate — plenty of room to lean into it or against it.
stage.masters: At this stage it's becoming a working style you can name and deliberately shape.
stage.phd: Through a PhD this tendency gets stress-tested daily; knowing it is half of managing it.
stage.postdoc: As a postdoc it's part of your signature — worth leaning on, and worth watching where it costs you.
stage.staff: In a research-staff role it's the reliability others quietly build on; protect what makes it work.
stage.pi: Leading a group, your tendency becomes the group's weather — it sets the tone whether you mean it to or not.
stage.industry: In industry it meets deadlines and stakeholders, which reward some of these leans and tax others.
stage.independent: Working independently, this tendency runs unchecked by a lab's habits — its own best and worst editor.

BAYES.name: Prior Alchemist
BAYES.description: You treat every result as evidence to fold into a running belief, not a verdict. Priors are your raw material; you distrust certainty that arrives too fast and love a posterior that shifts just enough.
strengths: Calibrates confidence to the actual evidence
Comfortable reasoning under uncertainty
Integrates messy signals into one coherent picture
blindSpots: Can over-tune priors until nothing surprises you
Sometimes hedges when a decision is needed
May assume everyone shares your probabilistic vocabulary
BAYES.teamRole: The one who asks 'but how sure are we, really?'
BAYES.reviewerTwo: You reply with a supplementary figure and a footnote on your assumptions.
BAYES.failureMode: Six weeks refining a prior for a dataset that never arrives.
BAYES.survivalAdvice: Set a decision deadline before you open the notebook.
BAYES.shareCard: I fold every result into a running belief.

PIPET.name: Precision Operator
PIPET.description: Your superpower is doing the same careful thing the same careful way, a thousand times. Protocols are sacred, labels are legible, and your error bars are small because your technique is not sloppy.
strengths: Exceptional consistency and tidy records
Spots drift before it becomes a disaster
Trusted with the fiddly, high-stakes steps
blindSpots: Can resist a better method because the old one works
Undersells the intellectual weight of the craft
Frustrated by collaborators who improvise
PIPET.teamRole: The steady hands everyone secretly depends on.
PIPET.reviewerTwo: You already have the extra replicate. You did it last month.
PIPET.failureMode: Perfecting a protocol nobody will run again.
PIPET.survivalAdvice: Write the protocol down so the whole lab inherits your precision.
PIPET.shareCard: The same careful thing, the same careful way.

R2D2.name: Reviewer Two Reincarnated
R2D2.description: You read every claim with a raised eyebrow and a pencil. Where others see a finished result, you see the three caveats it forgot. It's exhausting; it's also why the work survives contact with reality.
strengths: Finds the hole before the world does
Raises the rigour of everyone around you
Immune to a pretty figure with weak support
blindSpots: Scepticism can tip into obstruction
Hard to satisfy, easy to demoralise
May slow the momentum a project needs
R2D2.teamRole: The internal referee who saves the paper from itself.
R2D2.reviewerTwo: You ARE Reviewer 2. You leave the comment before it's even submitted.
R2D2.failureMode: A perfect critique of a project that therefore never ships.
R2D2.survivalAdvice: Aim your rigour at the claim, not the person making it.

FIG1.name: Diagram Architect
FIG1.description: You think in arrows and boxes. A clean schematic is how you understand the world — sometimes before every arrow is fully earned. The story comes first; the data are invited to agree.
strengths: Turns a tangle into a legible mechanism
Communicates ideas others can build on
Sees the shape of an argument early
blindSpots: May draw the arrow before the evidence exists
Attached to an elegant model past its expiry
Presentation can outrun proof
FIG1.teamRole: The one whose whiteboard becomes the paper's Figure 1.
FIG1.reviewerTwo: You redraw the diagram until the objection looks like a missing box.
FIG1.failureMode: A gorgeous schematic with a dashed line doing all the work.
FIG1.survivalAdvice: Label every arrow with the evidence that earns it.

NULL.name: Null Result Guardian
NULL.description: You defend the honest negative. A result that says 'no effect' is a result, and you refuse to let it vanish into a drawer. Your integrity is quiet and load-bearing.
strengths: Protects the record from publication bias
Comfortable reporting an unglamorous truth
Rigorous about what the data do and don't show
blindSpots: Can under-sell genuinely exciting findings
Risks framing everything as inconclusive
May clash with a hype-driven field
NULL.teamRole: The conscience of the results section.
NULL.reviewerTwo: You thank Reviewer 2 for confirming the effect really isn't there.
NULL.failureMode: A beautifully rigorous nothing that no venue wants.
NULL.survivalAdvice: Frame the null as the boundary it draws, not the absence it fills.

GIT.name: Version-Control Paladin
GIT.description: Your history is a clean, legible timeline anyone can walk back through. You believe reproducibility is a moral position, and 'it works on my machine' is the beginning of a confession, not an answer.
strengths: Everything is tracked, nothing is lost
Makes collaboration frictionless
Reproducible by default, not by heroics
blindSpots: Can bikeshed workflow over substance
Impatient with messy but productive colleagues
May over-engineer a two-file project
GIT.teamRole: The one who set up the repo the whole team relies on.
GIT.reviewerTwo: You link Reviewer 2 to the exact commit that answers them.
GIT.failureMode: A flawless pipeline for a result you never got around to finding.
GIT.survivalAdvice: Remember the commits are for the science, not the other way round.

EXCEL.name: Spreadsheet Sorcerer
EXCEL.tagline: A pivot table can hold a universe.
EXCEL.description: Give you a grid and a deadline and you'll conjure an answer. You are pragmatic, fast, and quietly powerful — the person who 'just made a quick sheet' that now runs half the lab.
strengths: Improvises analysis with whatever is at hand
Bridges people who don't speak statistics
Ships an answer while others set up their environment
blindSpots: The magic sheet has no version history
One typo can quietly poison everything
Reluctant to graduate to real tooling
EXCEL.teamRole: The one whose spreadsheet mysteriously became infrastructure.
EXCEL.reviewerTwo: You add a tab, a chart and a colour scale, and resubmit.
EXCEL.failureMode: A 40-tab workbook only you can operate, and you're on holiday.
EXCEL.survivalAdvice: Port the load-bearing sheet into something that can be tested.
EXCEL.shareCard: A pivot table can hold a universe.

OMNI.name: Ask-Everything Explorer
OMNI.tagline: But what if we also looked at…
OMNI.description: Curiosity is your engine and your hazard. Every dataset opens three new questions, and you want all of them. You range wide, connect distant ideas, and occasionally forget which project you started with.
strengths: Sees connections across distant fields
Generates ideas faster than anyone can use them
Fearless with unfamiliar methods
blindSpots: Breadth at the cost of depth
Many open tabs, few closed loops
Can scatter a team's focus
OMNI.teamRole: The idea fountain who needs a good closer nearby.
OMNI.reviewerTwo: You answer with two new analyses and a fresh hypothesis.
OMNI.failureMode: Nine half-finished side quests and no main story.
OMNI.survivalAdvice: Pick one thread and finish it before the next one glitters.
OMNI.shareCard: But what if we also looked at…

NIGHT.name: Deadline Nocturnalist
NIGHT.description: Your productivity runs on a different clock. Deadlines summon a strange lucidity, and the quiet of the small hours is where the real work happens — for better and for your sleep schedule.
strengths: Delivers brilliantly under pressure
Deep focus when the world goes quiet
Improvises fast when it counts
blindSpots: Boundaries between work and life have dissolved
Feast-or-famine rhythm is hard on collaborators
Runs on adrenaline and cold coffee
NIGHT.teamRole: The one who saves the submission at 3am.
NIGHT.reviewerTwo: You rebut at midnight, coherently, somehow.
NIGHT.failureMode: A brilliant sprint followed by a week of recovery.
NIGHT.survivalAdvice: Protect one boundary before it protects nothing.

REPRO.name: Reproducibility Inspector
REPRO.tagline: If it doesn't re-run, it didn't happen.
REPRO.description: You hold results to the standard of 'someone else can get this too'. You seed your randoms, pin your versions, and treat a result you can't reproduce as a bug, not a discovery.
strengths: Makes findings that actually hold up
Catches silent errors others miss
Raises the whole field's floor
blindSpots: Can prioritise re-running over discovering
Exacting standards can feel like distrust
Slows a team chasing a deadline
REPRO.teamRole: The one who re-runs it before anyone believes it.
REPRO.reviewerTwo: You send Reviewer 2 the seeded, containerised pipeline.
REPRO.failureMode: Reproducing a null so thoroughly there's no time left to extend it.
REPRO.survivalAdvice: Reproducibility serves discovery — don't let it replace it.
REPRO.shareCard: If it doesn't re-run, it didn't happen.

TODO.name: Eternal Experiment Extender
TODO.description: The project is never quite done, because there's always one more control, one more condition, one more clean-up. Your thoroughness is real; so is the risk that 'almost finished' becomes a permanent address.
strengths: Thorough in the best sense
Anticipates the objection before it lands
Rarely ships something half-baked
blindSpots: 'Done' is a moving target
Scope creep is a way of life
Perfect becomes the enemy of published
TODO.teamRole: The one whose project is 90% done, indefinitely.
TODO.reviewerTwo: You agree with Reviewer 2 and add the four experiments they'd have asked for next.
TODO.failureMode: A near-perfect study that never leaves the drive.
TODO.survivalAdvice: Define 'done' on day one and defend it.

ESC.name: Strategic Pivot Specialist
ESC.description: You read the winds early and change course before the ship hits the rocks. Sunk costs don't hold you; you'd rather pivot to the promising thing than defend the doomed one out of pride.
strengths: Cuts losses before they compound
Finds the viable path in a dead end
Adapts fast when the plan breaks
blindSpots: Can pivot away just before the payoff
Leaves a trail of nearly-finished work
Hard to tell resilience from restlessness
ESC.teamRole: The one who calls the pivot the day before it's obvious.
ESC.reviewerTwo: You reframe the whole paper around Reviewer 2's objection.
ESC.failureMode: Pivoting so often the destination becomes theoretical.
ESC.survivalAdvice: Give a promising direction long enough to actually pay off.

MODEL.name: Model-Universe Builder
MODEL.tagline: Everything is a system to simulate.
MODEL.description: You'd rather build the world than just observe it. Given a phenomenon, your instinct is to encode it — equations, simulations, predictions you can test. Reality is a benchmark for your model, not the other way round.
strengths: Makes intuition precise and testable
Predicts where data haven't gone yet
Sees the generative structure behind the noise
blindSpots: Can fall in love with the model over the world
Elegant assumptions can quietly be wrong
May over-fit a beautiful theory
MODEL.teamRole: The one who turns the hand-wave into equations.
MODEL.reviewerTwo: You add a sensitivity analysis and dare Reviewer 2 to break it.
MODEL.failureMode: A magnificent model of a system that behaves differently.
MODEL.survivalAdvice: Let the world veto the model, early and often.
MODEL.shareCard: Everything is a system to simulate.

GRANT.name: Funding Summoner
GRANT.tagline: I can turn a vision into a budget.
GRANT.description: You translate ambition into the language that pays for it. You see the arc from idea to impact and can make a panel believe it. The work runs because you keep the lights on.
strengths: Turns ideas into resourced projects
Reads what a room needs to hear
Keeps a whole group funded and moving
blindSpots: Vision can outrun the bench reality
Pitch mode can blur the caveats
Time on proposals is time off the data
GRANT.teamRole: The one who keeps the whole enterprise solvent.
GRANT.reviewerTwo: You thank Reviewer 2 and cite them in the next proposal.
GRANT.failureMode: A perfectly funded project no one has time to actually run.
GRANT.survivalAdvice: Promise what the data can plausibly deliver.
GRANT.shareCard: I turn a vision into a budget.

PREPRINT.name: Early-Release Sprinter
PREPRINT.description: You'd rather get the idea into the world now than polish it into next year. Speed is a feature; feedback is fuel. Perfect can wait — the conversation can't.
strengths: Moves fast and plants a flag
Invites feedback early, iterates in public
Rarely paralysed by polish
blindSpots: Occasionally ships before it's solid
Iterating in public can bite
Speed can outrun the caveats
PREPRINT.teamRole: The one who posts v1 while others draft the abstract.
PREPRINT.reviewerTwo: You already addressed Reviewer 2 — in version 3, last week.
PREPRINT.failureMode: A bold v1 claim that v2 has to quietly walk back.
PREPRINT.survivalAdvice: Ship fast, but be as loud about the caveats as the claim.

SOLO.name: Independent Research Hermit
SOLO.tagline: I think best with the door closed.
SOLO.description: You do your deepest work alone. Meetings drain you, but a quiet week with a hard problem restores you. You're self-directed to the core and answer mostly to your own standard.
strengths: Sustained deep focus without supervision
Owns a problem end to end
Unfazed by working in the dark
blindSpots: Under-communicates until it's done
Misses what a second pair of eyes would catch
Can drift without external checkpoints
SOLO.teamRole: The one who vanishes and returns with the whole thing solved.
SOLO.reviewerTwo: You quietly fix all six points and say nothing.
SOLO.failureMode: A brilliant solution to a problem the team had already dropped.
SOLO.survivalAdvice: Surface early — silence isn't the same as progress.
SOLO.shareCard: I think best with the door closed.

CONTROL.name: Control Condition Guardian
CONTROL.description: Your first question is always the control. You know a result means nothing without the right baseline, and you build comparisons others didn't think to include. Rigour, for you, starts before the experiment runs.
strengths: Designs the comparison that makes a result real
Catches confounds at the planning stage
Sceptical in the most constructive way
blindSpots: Can add controls until the study is unwieldy
Risk-averse when a leap is warranted
May over-plan a quick exploration
CONTROL.teamRole: The one who asks 'where's the baseline?' before you start.
CONTROL.reviewerTwo: You already ran the control Reviewer 2 wanted. Twice.
CONTROL.failureMode: A design so controlled there's no room left for a signal.
CONTROL.survivalAdvice: Control what matters; let the rest breathe.

PIPELINE.name: Workflow Tamer
PIPELINE.tagline: If I do it twice, I automate it.
PIPELINE.description: You turn chaos into a repeatable process. Manual steps offend you; a good pipeline is a gift to your future self and everyone downstream. You'd rather spend a day building the tool than a week running it by hand.
strengths: Automates the tedious into reliability
Scales a workflow the whole team can use
Removes human error by design
blindSpots: Can automate before the process is settled
Tooling can become its own project
Over-builds for a one-off need
PIPELINE.teamRole: The one whose pipeline the whole lab now runs on.
PIPELINE.reviewerTwo: You add a config flag and re-run everything for Reviewer 2 overnight.
PIPELINE.failureMode: A month automating a task that ran three times, ever.
PIPELINE.survivalAdvice: Automate the thing you've actually done twice — not once.
PIPELINE.shareCard: If I do it twice, I automate it.

PI.name: You Became the Supervisor
PI.description: Somewhere along the way your calendar filled with other people's projects. You steer, fund, and unblock more than you pipette or code. It's not a demotion from the science — it's a different instrument, played on people and priorities.
strengths: Sees the whole board, not just one square
Grows other researchers
Turns scattered effort into a direction
blindSpots: Hands are further from the data every year
Can mistake a meeting for progress
Nostalgic for the bench you rarely touch
PI.teamRole: The one who now asks the questions you used to dread.
PI.reviewerTwo: You forward Reviewer 2 to a student with 'good practice for you'.
PI.failureMode: A dozen projects supervised, none of them yours.
PI.survivalAdvice: Keep one small thing that's yours and hands-on.

GHOST.name: Meeting Invisibility Specialist
GHOST.description: You've mastered the art of being present without being reachable. It's not disengagement exactly — it's boundary-setting with a mute button. The work gets done; the meeting simply doesn't get you.
strengths: Guards focus fiercely
Immune to meeting-driven busywork
Delivers without needing an audience
blindSpots: The team loses your voice in the room
Decisions get made without your input
Invisibility can read as absence
GHOST.teamRole: The one whose contributions arrive by document, never by microphone.
GHOST.reviewerTwo: You never see Reviewer 2's comment; someone else handles it.
GHOST.failureMode: A great idea no one heard because your camera was off.
GHOST.survivalAdvice: Unmute for the one meeting that actually decides things.

POLISH.name: Presentation-First Perfectionist
POLISH.tagline: It's not done until it's beautiful.
POLISH.description: You feel the pull of a gorgeous figure before you've decided what it means. Aesthetics are a real strength — you make ideas legible and inviting — but the temptation is to let the polish arrive before the verdict. A fictional nudge, never a judgement on your integrity.
strengths: Makes complex work clear and compelling
Raises the presentation bar for everyone
Instinct for what an audience will grasp
blindSpots: Polish can outpace the analysis
A beautiful figure can flatter a thin result
The tempting shortcut is to decide by how good it looks
POLISH.teamRole: The one whose slides everyone borrows.
POLISH.reviewerTwo: You redesign the figure so elegantly Reviewer 2 forgets the question.
POLISH.failureMode: A stunning figure resting on a result you hadn't finished checking.
POLISH.survivalAdvice: Decide what's true first; make it beautiful second.
POLISH.shareCard: It's not done until it's beautiful.
